title: Chapter 1: I Quit (And Also I'm a Queen Now)
content: <!-- Speed: Sprint | D7+D10 -->

The fluorescent lights in Conference Room 3B were flickering. Not the normal kind of flickering — the kind that made Chloe Miller think of dying stars. Of ancient magic wards shorting out. Of *a realm in desperate need of its rightful ruler.*
She'd been at her desk for thirty-six hours.

Thirty-six hours of copy for a protein powder that tasted like chalk and disappointment. Thirty-six hours of her supervisor, Mark, sending emails at 3 AM that all began with "Quick thought — " and ended with her rewriting everything by sunrise. Thirty-six hours of staring at a motivational poster of a cat hanging from a branch that said *HANG IN THERE* and wanting to set it on fire.
And then, at hour thirty-five, Mark had stood up in the morning standup and presented her campaign deck as his own.
"Just a few slides I threw together last night," he'd said, while Chloe watched her own font choices, her own color palette, her own *soul* parade across the screen under his name.
She'd opened her UberEats. Cold noodles. Hair in the noodles.
That was hour thirty-six.
Something in Chloe's brain — a very specific something, possibly the last remaining screw — had unscrewed itself completely.
She stood up.
"Chloe?" Mark said. "We're still in standup."
Chloe reached under her desk. Her fingers closed around cool metal.
The sword.

She'd ordered it six months ago during a 2 AM depressive episode — a 1:1 replica of Andúril, Flame of the West. It had been sitting under her desk ever since, gathering dust and giving the cleaning staff existential crises. It cost $247. Best purchase of her life.
She drew the sword.
The conference room went silent.
"Mark," Chloe said, and her voice was different now. Deeper. Like someone who'd just remembered they were the main character. "Your dark reign over this department ends today."
Mark blinked. "My — what?"

"You are a Shadow Goblin of the lowest order," Chloe continued, "a creature that feeds on stolen creative energy. You cannot create. You can only consume. You are the final boss of this dungeon, and I — " she raised the sword, " — am the protagonist."
Someone in the back of the room started recording on their phone.

Mark's face cycled through five emotions in two seconds: confusion, indignation, fear, more confusion, and finally the desperate smile of a man who has realized he is being filmed and doesn't know whether to laugh or call security.
"Is this... a bit?" he asked. "For the culture committee?"
"The only culture here," Chloe said, "is a toxic one. And I am here to purify it with divine flame."

She did not have divine flame. But she did have a lighter she'd stolen from the company kitchen for "team bonding s'mores night" that had never happened because Mark had canceled it to "optimize Q3 bandwidth."
She flicked the lighter. The tiny flame danced.
Three people gasped. One person — Jenna from UX, who Chloe had always suspected was secretly cool — said "oh my god, finally" under her breath.
"I, Chloe Artemis Miller," Chloe announced, "hereby dissolve this branch of the Dark Empire and reclaim my rightful title as Queen of Avalon."
She reached into her bag and pulled out the plastic elf crown she'd bought on Temu for $3.49. She placed it on her head.
It was crooked. It was magnificent.
And in that moment — under flickering fluorescent lights that had never looked more like ancient dying wards — Chloe felt something she hadn't felt in two years.
Alive.
"Security has been called," Mark said, his voice cracking.
"Let them come," Chloe said. "I have faced far worse than your garrison of mall cops."
She turned to the conference room door — and there was Sam.

Sam Chen, corporate finance analyst, chronic anxiety sufferer, owner of the driest sense of humor west of the Mississippi and three Excel spreadsheets tracking her own cortisol levels. Sam was standing in the doorway holding Chloe's keyboard, neatly unplugged, and wearing the expression of someone who had seen this coming since approximately week one of their friendship.
"You forgot your keyboard," Sam said.
"I didn't forget it," Chloe said. "I was leaving it as a warning."
"A warning."
"Like a severed horse head. But ergonomic."

Sam took a long sip of her iced americano. The sip lasted approximately four seconds, which in Sam-time was an eternity — it meant she was processing something deeply. Chloe knew this. She'd catalogued all of Sam's silences over the years: the three-second sip (mild disapproval), the five-second sip (genuine concern), and the rare seven-second sip (she was about to do something completely irrational in the name of friendship).
This was a four-second sip. Right in the danger zone.
"So," Sam said, setting down her americano. "We're doing this."
"We're doing this."
"Right now."
"Right now."

Sam looked at the sword. She looked at the crooked plastic crown. She looked at Mark, who had retreated to the corner of the conference room and appeared to be praying. She looked at the twenty-three junior employees who were all holding up their phones.
"Fine," Sam said. "But I'm driving."

And that was the thing about Sam Chen. She would give you a forty-five-minute PowerPoint presentation on why your plan was insane, with citations, appendices, and a risk assessment matrix color-coded in three shades of red. And then she would hand you the keys.
Chloe felt something crack open in her chest. Not break — crack open. Like a window in a room that had been sealed for years.
"To the parking garage," Chloe said, raising her sword. "Shadowfax awaits."
"It's a 2008 Honda Civic," Sam said.
"Shadowfax," Chloe repeated. "The Lord of Horses."
"The AC leaks on the passenger side."
"A noble steed bears its battle scars with dignity."

Sam sighed — a sigh that contained approximately three years of accumulated exasperation — and started walking.
Ch1EOF3

They walked through the open-plan office — Chloe in front, sword held high, plastic crown catching the fluorescent light like it was actual starlight; Sam three steps behind, holding Chloe's keyboard under one arm and her iced americano in the other hand.
Every desk they passed, people stopped typing.
"Is she... okay?" someone whispered.
"She hasn't slept in thirty-six hours," Sam said, without breaking stride. "And Mark stole her deck. She's more okay than she's been in two years."
The elevator doors opened. Chloe stepped in, turned to face the office one last time, and raised her sword in salute.
"To the employees of this dungeon," she declared. "You are not goblins. You are prisoners. And one day — " the doors began to close, " — you too shall remember who you are."
The doors shut.
In the sudden silence of the elevator, Chloe's sword arm began to tremble.
Sam noticed. Sam noticed everything.
"You okay?" Sam asked. The question was casual, but Chloe heard the real question underneath: *Do you need me to catch you right now?*

Chloe lowered the sword. She stared at her reflection in the polished elevator doors — a 23-year-old woman in a Temu crown, holding a replica sword, wearing yesterday's clothes, with circles under her eyes so dark they looked like bruises.
She looked ridiculous.
She looked free.
"I'm better than okay," Chloe said. "I'm the protagonist."
Sam took a sip of her americano. Three seconds. Mild disapproval.
"The protagonist needs to eat something," Sam said. "When's the last time you had a meal that wasn't cold noodles with hair in it?"
"The hair was a sign," Chloe said. "A message from the universe."
"What did the message say?"
"'Stop eating cold noodles.'"
Sam almost smiled. Almost. The corner of her mouth twitched upward approximately 0.3 millimeters — which, for Sam, was the equivalent of a standing ovation.

The parking garage was on B2, which Chloe immediately reclassified as "the Royal Stables, Second Subterranean Level." The fluorescent tubes down here were even worse than the ones upstairs — half of them were out, and the ones that worked buzzed with the low, persistent hum of dying electronics.
Shadowfax sat in spot 47.

It was a 2008 Honda Civic. The paint had once been silver but had faded to a color that could best be described as "giving up." The rear bumper was covered in anime stickers: a chibi cat holding a sword, a glowing rune from a video game Sam didn't recognize, and one that said in small block letters: IF YOU CAN READ THIS RUNE YOU'RE TOO CLOSE.
The check engine light had been on since 2021.
"There she is," Chloe breathed. "The Lord of Horses."
"The AC leaks," Sam said, unlocking the doors. "And it makes a noise above sixty-five that sounds like a dying whale."

"True power never comes without cost," Chloe said, climbing into the passenger seat. She arranged her sword across her lap, adjusted her crown, and rolled down the window — which, unlike the AC, worked perfectly, because the universe had a sense of humor.

Sam settled into the driver's seat. She set her americano in the cup holder (permanently sticky from a 2022 Sprite incident), plugged in her phone (the aux cord had been severed in 2023, repaired with electrical tape, severed again, and was now held together by sheer willpower), and pulled up the playlist she'd made three weeks ago when she'd first suspected this day was coming.
It was called "Chloe's Breakdown Road Trip."
Track one: "Mr. Brightside."
"You made a playlist," Chloe said.

"I made a playlist," Sam confirmed. "I also made an Excel spreadsheet of rest stops, motels under eighty dollars a night with ratings above three stars, and gas stations with the cleanest bathrooms on the 101 corridor."
Chloe stared at her.
"You knew."

"Chloe." Sam turned the key. The engine coughed twice, shuddered, and came to life with the enthusiasm of a retiree being asked to run a marathon. "I've known since the day you brought that sword to the office. I've just been waiting for the hair in the noodles."
"The hair was the final sign."
"The hair was unsanitary."
"Same thing."

Sam pulled out of spot 47. The tires squeaked on the concrete — not in a cool way, in a "these tires should have been replaced in 2020" way. They spiraled up the parking garage ramp, emerging into the gray San Francisco afternoon like a submarine breaking surface.
The city spread out before them: glass towers, crawling traffic, a fog bank rolling in from the bay like the breath of some ancient sleeping creature.
Chloe leaned her head out the window. The wind hit her face — cold, damp, tasting of salt and exhaust and something else she couldn't name. Something that felt like *possibility*.
She took a deep breath.
And then she screamed.
Not a scared scream. Not an angry scream. A *battle cry* — the kind of scream you let out when you've been holding your breath for two years and suddenly remember your lungs work.
"AVALON!" she screamed into the San Francisco wind. "YOUR QUEEN RETURNS!"

A man on the sidewalk looked up from his phone. A pigeon startled and took flight. A Tesla in the next lane honked — probably at Sam's driving, probably at Chloe's screaming, probably at the sheer audacity of two young women in a dying Honda Civic acting like they owned the world.
Sam didn't flinch. Sam didn't smile. But Sam turned up the volume on "Mr. Brightside" until the speakers rattled.
And she drove south.
Three hours later, somewhere past San Jose, Chloe turned to Sam and said, completely seriously: "You know what I just realized?"
"That you forgot your laptop?"

"No. Well, yes. But also — " Chloe gestured at the highway ahead, at the taillights glowing red in the dusk like scattered embers, at the 2008 Honda Civic that was definitely making a new noise she hadn't heard before, at the woman beside her who had abandoned her entire life on a Thursday afternoon because her best friend had declared herself a monarch. " — this is a really good story."
"It's a breakdown," Sam said.
"Same thing," Chloe said.
And then she looked directly at you.
Yes, *you*.

She couldn't actually see you — you're reading a book, or scrolling on your phone, or maybe listening to the audiobook while pretending to work. But Chloe tilted her head, squinted slightly, and said: "Hi. Thanks for reading. Stick around — it gets weirder."
Sam didn't even look over. "Are you talking to the readers again?"
"Someone has to."
"No one has to."
"The author's not doing it."
"There is no author. We're in a Honda Civic."
"Exactly," Chloe said. "Which makes this a really, really good story."
Sam took a four-second sip of her americano.
And kept driving.
